text: In lithium metal anodes (LMAs) operating at >95% Coulombic efficiency, the formation and growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) represent the primary drivers of capacity loss. Despite its importance, the fundamental mechanisms governing this degradation remain elusive. A critical factor influencing SEI evolution is its inherent solubility within the electrolyte. In this study, we utilize *in operando* electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance (EQCM) to systematically quantify and compare the solubility of SEIs formed in LMA-optimized ether-based electrolytes. Our findings establish a clear correlation between SEI solubility, passivity, and long-term cyclability, demonstrating that dissolution is a dominant factor in the performance variations observed across different battery chemistries. By integrating EQCM data with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analysis, we reveal that SEI stability is a function of both interphase composition and the chemical properties of the bulk electrolyte. This work provides essential insights for designing more stable interfaces, offering a strategic pathway to minimize capacity loss during the cycling and aging of high-performance batteries.
